title: Fitzpatrick's Harbour Town Masterclass: RBC Heritage Playoff Triumph
excerpt: Matt Fitzpatrick defeats world No. 1 Scottie Scheffler in a dramatic playoff at Harbour Town to claim his second RBC Heritage title and fourth PGA Tour win.
body:
Fitzpatrick Survives Late Wobble to Claim Playoff Victory

Matt Fitzpatrick proved that composure under pressure remains golf's greatest virtue on Sunday at Harbour Town Golf Links, downing world No. 1 Scottie Scheffler in a sudden-death playoff after squandering a three-shot lead down the stretch. The English precision-player, 32, won his second RBC Heritage title and fourth PGA Tour victory with a composed 13-foot birdie putt on the 18th in the playoff decider, claiming the $3.6m winner's cheque.

Winning Score
18 Under
Fitzpatrick PGA Wins
4
First Prize
$3.6m
World Ranking Points
48

When Control Slipped Away

Fitzpatrick entered Sunday with a commanding lead, stretching it to four shots inside the first three holes. Seemingly in control with 14 straight pars and only four holes remaining, the script flipped dramatically. Back-to-back birdies from Scheffler at holes 15 and 16 set the stage. A bogey at the par-four 18th saw Fitzpatrick slip back to 18 under—the very mark Scheffler would reach moments later. Harbour Town's tight, wind-exposed layout punished any lapse in concentration, and both men found themselves locked in at 266, 18 under par.

The Playoff Moment

The playoff returned to the 18th, the par-four playing hard into the wind. Fitzpatrick's second shot found the green from 8 feet below the hole. Scheffler, starting from a fairway lie, could not find the putting surface in two and was left needing to get up and down. His pitch finished short, and the birdie putt that didn't fall left Fitzpatrick needing just his two putts to seal it. The first birdie putt dropped from 13 feet, and with it, a second Heritage trophy went to Fitzpatrick's collection.

Scheffler's Stranglehold Loosens

For all his dominance this season, Scheffler's failure to close out on Sunday proved costly. The world No. 1 had shown control through 72 holes, but Harbour Town's demanding finishing stretch exposed a vulnerability—perhaps the one thing every great champion fears: the sudden loss of momentum. Si Woo Kim rounded out the top three at 16 under, his best PGA Tour finish in months.

What's Next

The PGA Tour moves to Quail Hollow next week for the Wells Fargo Championship. Fitzpatrick, now riding an enormous wave of confidence after a second RBC Heritage title, will head there among the favourites. Scheffler will look to reset and regroup before the calendar turns to major championships. For golf fans, the message is clear: complacency has no place at the game's highest level, no matter how dominant your play has been.
